title: Part 15
text: expression of Eric Voegelin’s, so dominant in all variants of positivist thought. The visions of modern thinkers were always based on the claim that an absolute and positive truth about the order of the world would bring certainty to the action of man and lead the world into some kind of new realm. Like the Greek titans, they are sublimations of the image of the self-reliant shamanistic trickster. From the seventeenth century the transcendent realm had manifest itself as this-worldly truth. Hobbes and Descartes can be identified as critical key figures in this process. The notion of salvation was still driving their scientific thought. In the systems proposed by Hobbes and Descartes, secularization ultimately came to mean a possession of salvation in the here and now. As Voegelin warned (Voegelin 2004) this led to the ultimate kind of positivism, where man takes the place of God. Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 139 On that note it is certainly true that ‘absolutism’ in state organization and human knowledge is a myth, and possibly the greatest of all impossibilities. But myths have to be taken seriously in their very quality of myths. This chapter has argued that the origin of the myth of absolute knowledge and absolute statehood is religious. The idea of an absolute power over all others, that has no other source but its own power, that has no equal, no superior: this is the idea of God. It is the idea of God manifesting itself directly in the this-worldly realm, and it can be nothing other than that. There is nothing in individual sensory experience that can foster the idea of the absolute. On this point Descartes was right. The experience of an absolute power existing over and above us is transcendental, whatever the origin. The idea was transposed and applied to the level of state and self so as to deny the very reality of the transcendental experience, or so as to rule out its validity – and triumphantly so by Hobbes and Descartes. God was brought so close to rational man that he became identical with him, and so with time superfluous. When a state becomes represented as the ultimate condition of its own existence, and the human self as the ultimate condition of its own truth, then the religious realm has not been left to itself: it has been laid down, taken full possession of. Divinity has been turned into the condition of empirical being. With Voegelin’s words, this means that we have entered an age of gnosticism, an age characterized by immanentist versions of a secularized eschatology. As we shall discuss further in Chapter 8, it was this worldview that came to underpin modern political revolutions. However, to the extent that this was a process of rationalization, it happened alongside novel forms of playfulness that were not simply obliterated by reason and order. In fact, the gaming and gambling spirit, and the entire quest for adrenalin experiences which is today so dominant developed alongside scientific and political rationalization. To this aspect of liminality in the modern we can now turn. This page has been left blank intentionally Chapter 6 Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity I assert that what is serious should be treated seriously, and what is not serious should not, and that by nature god is worthy of a complete blessed seriousness, but that what is human, as we said earlier, has been devised as a certain plaything (paivgnion) of god, and that this is really the best thing about it. Every man and woman should spend life in this way, playing (paivzonta) the most beautiful games (paidiavı) (Plato, The Laws) Adventure has the gesture of the conqueror, the quick seizure of opportunity ... [yet also] the complete self-abandonment to the powers and accidents of the world, which can delight us, but in the same breath can also destroy us. (Georg Simmel, The Adventurer) Whenever we are seized with vertigo at the ceaseless shuttlings and spinnings in our mind of the thought: What is play? What is serious? we shall find the fixed, unmoving point that logic denies us, once more in the sphere of ethics. Play, we began by saying, lies outside morals. In itself it is neither good nor bad. But if we have to decide whether an action to which our will impels us is a serious duty or is licit as play, our moral conscience will at once provide the touchstone. As soon as truth and justice, compassion and forgiveness have part in our resolve to act, our anxious question loses all meaning. One drop of pity is enough to lift our doing beyond intellectual distinctions. Springing as it does from a belief in justice and divine grace, conscience, which is moral awareness, will always whelm the question that eludes and deludes us to the end, in a lasting silence. (Johan Huizinga, closing lines of Homo Ludens) I would like to start this chapter with an anecdote. In 2009 I moved with my family from the countryside to a neighbourhood in Rome. At that time, there were two small betting shops, and they were easy to spot because of the men constantly hanging around outside their doors, discussing possible outcomes of this or that horse race. Bringing my children to school in the morning morning, I would normally stop for a cappuccino at the local bar, right in front of the school. In 2009 there was one slot machine in the back room where I would skim the daily newspaper, trying to wake up properly. In 2012 there were three of those machines; a fourth, smaller betting machine was then squeezed in next to the counter in the front room. In the same period, the selection of scratch cards vastly expanded. Liminality and the Modern 142 The same thing happened in most other bars in the neighbourhood, which all of a sudden had turned into quasi-gambling houses – where coffee and cornetti also happened to be served. It is a quite astonishing development, considering the importance of such places for everyday sociability in a country like Italy. In fact, one would notice how an increasing number of persons entering the bar would not order anything to drink or eat at all, would not greet anyone with a ‘Good morning, how are you?’, as people normally do in Italy, but would instead go straight to the gambling machines and start dropping in coins. Maybe they did so because the real betting shops would only open later in the morning. By 2013 there were nine of those betting shops in my neighbourhood. This development is not confined to an anecdote from my neighbourhood; it is not confined to Rome, or Italy, or even Europe. It is happening everywhere and all the time, day and night. In Italy, as in many other European countries, gambling started to spread during the 1990s, in the general context of deregulation, 1 and the growth of the phenomenon has accompanied the spread of computers. I would sometimes wonder about this phenomenon as I made my way to the office. Turning on the computer, the wonder frequently turned into irritation, as offers of internet gambling kept popping up in small windows inside my Yahoo email account: ‘Win 1 million euro today!’. It has happened more than once that such windows would block my screen; when I subsequently, in perfect ignorance, clicked on the ‘close window’ symbol, I was instantly carried into one of those virtual gambling websites where everything is possible, or so they claim. Gambling practices have spread like a virus, penetrating all public and private spheres. Parents around the world have slowly started to worry, although, arguably, much less than we should. In Italy, where, as we shall see, modern gambling has its origin, it was attempted in 2012 at the political level to establish at least some minimum demarcations, prohibiting gambling houses and gambling machines in close proximity to schools, hospitals and churches. The attempt was in vain, as any such initiative was judged a violation of free trade and business. In several Italian cities, groups of married women started to protest and demand political control of the phenomenon: their husbands had all caught the ‘gambling bug’, and it was ruining their families. But it has all been to no avail. Gambling marches on, unstoppable. Most people reject permanent entry into this chaotic, passion- driven world and prefer to sample it only during brief visits to casinos or as they now and again click their way to (mis)fortune on the internet. But some people can’t resist gambling. They get caught up in endless gaming. It would be convenient if we could categorize such practices, and the people engaging them, as belonging to ‘deviant behaviour’ – as so much of the literature on gambling does with respect to gambling addicts. This, however, will not 1 The Italian state started to adopt new measures towards gambling from 1992. Italy risked default for its inability to service the public debt and the government recognized in gambling a fiscal policy measure. It helped the country into the Eurozone. Between 1992 and 2013, total gambling expenditure has increased approximately 800 per cent. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 143 really help us to capture what is going on. Our focus here is not on ‘problem gamblers’ or ‘addicts’; what we need to understand is not only how individuals lose control and must resort to medical treatment for their illness; the pathology is not (only) in that deviance – the pathology unfolds from within the heart of the very conditions of meaningful sociability. What is going on? Why is gambling spreading like wildfire? Where does it come from? And how are we to understand it? These are the questions we must discuss. While Victor Turner, in his essay, Liminal to Liminoid, did mention gambling, and also noticed its shared etymology with the notion of play (Turner 1982 [1974]), he never ventured into any analysis of it. This chapter, therefore, engages in a discussion concerning the spread of gaming and gambling in contemporary culture. The proliferation of gambling is one of the most evident ways in which liminal practices are moving to the core of everyday practices. Relatedly, and significantly, games are now proliferating among children who – and this is no minor detail – routinely carry around their own small ‘game- boxes’, enabling them to play anywhere they go. Many of these games that we let our children play at a still younger age are about killing; the player gains points for each time they manage to eliminate a person or a figure. They quickly become very skilled in their killing techniques. Game producers target children at a still younger age. The race to the bottom was (quite literally) reached when the ‘iPotty’ was introduced at the Consumer Electronics Show in early 2013, training children to deal with their natural functions by interacting with a gaming screen attached to the potty. Plato discussed the role of children’s play in a crucial passage in what is arguably the most crucial of all pieces, The Laws. He would not have hesitated one second to diagnose this phenomenon as a disease of soul undermining our political existence. In fact, the starting premise of Plato’s analysis is that life in the city has become utterly corrupted. The masses are being driven by the relentless pursuit of the pleasure principle, and have lost any distinction between what is good and bad. The populace of the polis has turned into a theatre-going audience waiting for the next ‘kick’ experience. It is this diagnosis of an emerging ‘theatrocracy’ that Szakolczai elaborates in full detail in his recent work (2013, see in particular pp. 30–33) on the genealogy of the public sphere. Plato argues that human beings can (and should) order their play with rhythm and melody, which must direct the choric education of children. 2 In this last and conclusive writing, Plato re- grounds his entire epistemology in children’s play: a healthy culture of the polis can only grow out of the balanced and musical play of children. But here they must be guided, because children, as we know, may take pleasure both in what is good and what is bad. Children, says Plato, must learn to take pleasure in what is good. That is the role of education, and that is the touchstone for Plato’s mature 2 On the role of liminality and the trickster’s game in education, and the formation of the participant as a ‘critical outsider’ (here with reference to introductory texts in sociology), see Boland 2010. Liminality and the Modern 144 political philosophy. A sense for the good, the just and the beautiful must develop together. The necessary affinities that (should) exist in our attitudes to the playful and the upbringing of children are again driven home by Greek etymology, in the tight connections between child (pais), playing (paidia) and culture (paideia). The laws, says Plato, are based on harmonious play; and the laws, the nomoi, are themselves like music. The fact that we have forgotten this touchstone does not diminish its relevance; quite the contrary. In what follows, it will of course be impossible to engage with the details of today’s enormous gambling or gaming industry in all its ordinary, pathological dimensions. There are entire libraries dedicated to the phenomenon of gambling, which is far from strange, considering the fact that it is one of the world’s biggest industries. We live in a world of constant gaming and constant gambling; that is the way it is, and it needs no further comment. What will be suggested is that here also, the diagnostic term proposed in previous chapters comes into its own, for this is one more sign that we are evidently plunging into an abyss of permanent liminality. But how did this happen? On the Religious Origins of ‘Games’ of Chance It is important to start with the fact that games of chance are not new to human beings, and as such do not belong specifically to modernity. Games of chance were up until very recently not at all related to money. Alfred Kroeber (1920) noticed that gambling-like activities took place even among peoples who have no notion of personal wealth. In his work on games among the Californian Indians, he included games of chance as one of five main types of game. Kroeber also noticed something quite crucial about the spread of gambling: not that it spread via specific games that were adopted across cultures, but that what was spread was much more like an attitude, an ethos – or, what, following Weber, we might also call a gambling ‘spirit’. It is this hidden spirit of the modern that needs to be retrieved. Archaeologists have found six-sided dice from around 3000 bc in present-day Iraq. And there is no doubt that four-sided dice existed for millennia before that. It is difficult to say with certainty how such dice were used 5000 years ago, but moving just a little closer to the present this difficulty disappears: devices used today in games of chance were originally used for making decisions during religious rituals (Brenner and Brenner 1990: 1). Drawing lots was used as a method to discover God’s will. One famous example is found in Leviticus (16: 6–10), in the context of the The Day of Atonement, where it reads: 6 Aaron is to offer the bull for his own sin offering to make atonement for himself and his household. 7 Then he is to take the two goats and present them before the L ord at the entrance to the tent of meeting. 8 He is to cast lots for the two goats – one lot for the L ord and the other for the scapegoat. 9 Aaron shall bring Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 145 the goat whose lot falls to the L ord and sacrifice it for a sin offering. 10 But the goat chosen by lot as the scapegoat shall be presented alive before the L ord to be used for making atonement by sending it into the wilderness as a scapegoat. 3 There are plenty of other examples in the Old Testament. Leaders and office holders were evidently appointed by drawing lots, as when Saul was selected to be King of Israel. When Jonah was thrown into the sea, it was because the lot fell on him, and so on. Lottery practices are widespread in a variety of cultures of which we have record. Throughout the world, and from the smallest to the most complex of societies, people have been using all sorts of devices (stones, sticks, leaves, arrows) to cast lots in bringing about a decision. In the Iliad, Zeus used a sacred balance to decide whether the Trojans or the Greeks would win the battle. In Greek mythology, the Gods cast lots to divide the universe among them, and that is how Zeus got the sky, Poseidon the seas and Hades the underworld. Hades lost in the lottery. However, and this is important to stress, casting lots in all these contexts was about making decisions in matters of religion, politics and law. And for the involved persons, such decisions were probably never really perceived as involving ‘chance’ at all, at least not as we have come to understand that term today; rather, casting lots was simply a modality or technique to find or bring about the will of the higher powers. The result of casting the lot was a technical means of uncovering destiny – and in fact, we still use the word ‘lot’ as synonymous with ‘destiny’. The notion of ‘pure chance’ is arguably only possible within a thoroughly disenchanted universe, as Dostoyevsky captured so tellingly in his novel, The Gambler (see Scanlan 2009). Therefore, the introduction of institutionalized, money-oriented games of chance needs to be accounted for. Anthropological Foundations and the Role of Play in Civilization We want to discuss games of chance, but we must keep in mind that any analysis of such games must be placed within the wider context of ‘play’ and the role of the playful in human evolution and contemporary culture. In terms of overall categorization, one can here refer to the work of Roger Caillois who usefully divided games into four main categories: agon, alea, mimicry and illinx (Caillois 2001: 12). Caillois’s classification follows from the degree of presence in each of them of three main elements: competition, chance and simulation. Agon covers sports and games in which the player actively attempts to direct the outcome of the contest or game (chess being an obvious example). Alea is 3 There is perhaps an extent to which this actually presents an empirical challenge to Girard’s theory of the scapegoat, for in the biblical text there is little trace of the social mechanisms of identifying the victim; Girard’s ‘persecuting crowd’ seems replaced by a lottery. Liminality and the Modern 146 primarily characterized by the element of chance. The player is (relatively at least) passive and surrenders to destiny. The most evident examples of alea are lotteries, roulette and dice games. Mimicry is characterized by the involvement of fantasy and suspension of reality on the part of the participant. Children’s games are the primary examples here. Lastly, ilinx involves an element of vertigo, gymnastic activities, skiing and mountain climbing being examples. Significantly, Caillois identifies ilinx by a ‘rapid whirling or falling movement’ (ibid.). In the following chapter we will discuss ilinx with reference to exactly such a ‘whirling-falling’, exemplified by bungee jumping practices. Here, however, it is primarily with alea, games of chance, that we are concerned. Categories aside, it must always be kept in mind that games of chance are a subcategory of play, and cannot be easily disentangled from the general evolution of the playful in civilization. This opens up a theme so broad that it can hardly be dealt with within one book, let alone a single chapter. Luckily we have good guides at our service, not least Johan Huizinga (1872–1945), the eminent Dutch historian, whose most famous work was of course Homo Ludens, and which elegantly laid out the underlying features of play, on the basis of which Caillois refined and formulated the above distinctions. Johan Huizinga and Norbert Elias on Play Civilization gradually brings about a certain division between two modes of mental life which we distinguish as play and seriousness respectively, but which originally formed a continuous mental medium wherein that civilization arose. (Johan Huizinga, Homo Ludens) As a thinker, Huizinga was always well-prepared to engage with the concept of play. Huizinga was trained as a linguist, and wrote his doctoral thesis on the role of the clown figure (or jester) in Sanskrit drama in 1897, before he turned to the study of Renaissance and early modern history. In all of his works, art, ritual and spectacle were identified as crucial; he perceived how artistic and ‘aestethic forms’ went to the heart of social life and attitudes, much more so than any socio- economic indicator. That is why he is seen as a founding father of cultural history. Although he was an close contemporary of Arnold van Gennep (who was born just four months later than Huizinga) he never encountered his work. Huizinga is famous for three works: In the The Waning of the Middle Ages he famously reinterpreted the later Middle Ages as a period of pessimism and decadence rather than rebirth. In 1924 he published a book on Erasmus (a non-player if ever there was one), whereas Homo Ludens appeared in 1938. The timing of the publication must be noted. Although Huizinga in Homo Ludens is not very explicit concerning the drama of the contemporary scene, there is no doubt that the motivating energy behind the book was a recognition that something had gone terribly wrong with the element of play in culture, and that this ‘something’ was connected to the rise of mass movements and totalitarian regimes, and to a profound cultural crisis – one from which we certainly cannot declare Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 147 immunity today. In other writings he was more explicit about these connections. In 1938 Huizinga also became vice-president of the International Committee of Intellectual Cooperation with the League of Nations. Alarmed by the rise of fascism and the spread of a deep-bound cultural crisis, Huizinga had written In de schaduwen van morgen (1935) which his son Jacob Herman Huizinga translated into English and published in 1936 under the title In the Shadow of Tomorrow. He was then working on Homo Ludens. The Dutch subtitle was A diagnosis of the spiritual suffering [leiden] of our times. The first chapter, Apprehensions of Doom, opens with these lines: We live in a demented [bezeten] world. And we know it. It would not come as a surprise to anyone if tomorrow the madness gave way to a frenzy which would leave our poor Europe in a state of distracted stupor, with engines still turning and flags streaming in the breeze, but with the spirit gone. [...] Everywhere there are doubts as to the solidity of our social structure, vague fears of the imminent future, a feeling that our civilization is on the way to ruin. (Huizinga 1936: 15) Evidently, Huizinga wrote Homo Ludens with the consciousness that old Europe was approaching its end, in a highly liminal moment. He was translating it into English and adding material right after the Nazis invaded Poland. He clearly intuited similarities with the psycho-social abendnacht moods during the ‘waning’ of the Middle Ages and the existential uncertainty and spread of violence that marked the present age. 4 Huizinga sensed a proliferating decay, and it was spreading even from within the two highly celebrated institutional achievements of modernity, namely universal education and the public sphere: Our time [is] faced by the discouraging fact that two highly vaunted achievements of civilization, universal education and modern publicity, instead of raising the level of culture, appear ultimately to produce certain symptoms of cultural devitalisation and degeneration (ibid.: 78). There are not a few parallels with the life and work of Norbert Elias, and they go far beyond the mere fact that Elias finished The Civilizing Process just a few months after Huizinga’s masterpiece. Both Huizinga and Elias realized the urgent need to understand, historically, what was happening with ‘culture’. Chapter IV in In The Shadow of Tomorrow is an attempt to identify Fundamentals of Culture (its title). However, rather than going straight to the substance, Huizinga – just like Elias – enters a discussion of the etymology of the word ‘culture’ and how it has developed within the variety of European languages, with a focus on German. 4 In 1941 Huizinga gave a speech in which he criticized German influences on Dutch science and was arrested by the Nazis. He was released in 1942 but not allowed to return to Leiden. Huizinga died in detention at De Steeg in Gerderland, near Arnheim, on 1 February 1945. Liminality and the Modern 148 Also, Huizinga takes notice of the common root in Greek of the words for culture (paideia) and play (paidia); a discussion which greatly inspired Voegelin, and which he incorporated in his own, very central, discussion of Plato’s The Laws (1974). In The Civilizing Process Elias took inspiration from Huizinga’s work, especially Erasmus and The Waning of the Middle Ages. Huizinga’s rendering of ordinary social relations and the psychic, inter-personal aspects of life in the Middle Ages are clearly present in Elias’ analysis, and surely more so than the brevity of Elias’ direct references to Huizinga may indicate. As van Krieken argues (1998: 23), Elias’ work is in fact best understood when read alongside Huizinga. It is also no coincidence that the element of play in culture and leisure was one of the explicit research topics later taken up by Elias, together with Eric Dunning. Elias surely understands the pivotal role of play and ritual; but does he actually theorize it well? Elias never says it this way, but if one considers what actually goes in within the European court cultures, pace his own description, the entire development can best be described as a ceremonial stifling of the element of play (Burke 2009), an excessive culture of play in which even the absolute King comes to stand powerless, caught in his own ceremonial game. 5 Caillos himself indicates something similar in his table summing up the corruption of games: carnival and theatre are marginal cultural forms of mimicry, while uniforms and ceremonial etiquette exemplify institutional forms of mimicry and simulation integrated into social life (Caillois 2001: 54). Alienation and split personality are the ultimate corrupted forms of mimicry at the levels of self and life-conduct. In his discussion of Elias, Mennell sums up how the aristocracy in court society became ‘specialists in the arts of consumption entrapped in a system of fine distinctions, status battles, and competitive expenditure from which they could not escape because their whole social identity depended upon it’ (Mennell as quoted in Featherstone 1992: 277). What developed was a still more tight and narrow frame for behaviour, dress and manners (Elias 1994: Part Two). Far from a genuine ‘civilizing process’ of deepening subjectivity and differentiation of consciousness (as Voegelin would have it), far from an expansion of human horizons within an experience of the transcendent (as Huizinga would have it), the court culture that Elias identifies as pivotal for modern rationality should rather be seen as a ritual passage stuck in the liminal phase: an endless game of strategic calculation, a continuous testing, a permanent use of exaggerated paraphernalia, funny hats and socks that are never taken off: an entrapment in a game that never ceases. It is arguably this stifled spirit and fossilized play-element that via the European court culture somehow came to permeate modern bourgeois rationality. A central part of the gaming culture that emerges in court culture has to do with role-playing; and it is Elias’ argument that it is this kind of role-playing that spreads beyond the walls of court culture. 5 It must be noted that in this structured positioning, the absolute King in many ways resembles today’s celebrities. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 149 Huizinga, Voegelin and Bateson: Framing Play In his analysis of play, Huizinga starts from anthropological foundations. In other words, he starts from Plato. In his assessment of Huizinga, Voegelin (1948) appreciates how Huizinga’s work is actually not ‘new’; what Huizinga does it to retrieve a theme from classical philosophy which had been lost, or which had become muddled. 6 Play is crucial for social, cognitive and emotional development. It matters for how we feel, for how we think, and for how we live together as human beings in a meaningful way as part of a social environment. Without play there can be no culture, no knowledge, no sentiment. Play is literally fundamental ‘in the sense that it cannot be reduced to another factor. Neither must play be interpreted in a utilitarian manner as serving a purpose, nor must its meaning be derived from the content that it presents; either attempt would destroy the independent meaning of play’ (Voegelin 1974: 311). For Voegelin as for Huizinga, it is the non-utilitarian character of play, music and rhythm that allows it to become ‘a source for the creation of new worlds of meaning beyond the everyday world’ (ibid.). Voegelin follows up on Huizinga’s presentation, stressing the way that play is an ‘overflow’ beyond the ordinary, normal existence: it is a central source of transcendence, and thus a vehicle of cultural growth; or, to use a different terminology, play has liminal features, existing in the world of the in-between, opening up new horizons – if treated carefully. And that is why we have to take it seriously. Play existed before Homo Sapiens –
